title: Understanding the Fundamental Group
text:
In algebraic topology, one of the most basic invariants used to study topological spaces is the fundamental group. It captures information about the 1-dimensional “holes” in a space by studying the behaviour of loops.

Intuition: Loops on a Surface
Imagine placing a loop of string on a surface: * On a sphere (like a football), any loop you draw can be shrunk down continuously to a single point. * On a torus (the surface of a donut), a loop that wraps around the tube or around the central hole cannot be shrunk to a point without cutting the surface or the string.
This distinction is formalized by the fundamental group, denoted \(\pi_1(X, x_0)\).


Formal Definition
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(x_0 \in X\) be a basepoint. A loop based at \(x_0\) is a continuous map \(f: [0, 1] \to X\) such that \(f(0) = f(1) = x_0\).
Two loops \(f\) and \(g\) are homotopic (written \(f \simeq g\)) if there exists a continuous map (a homotopy) \(H: [0, 1] \times [0, 1] \to X\) such that: * \(H(s, 0) = f(s)\) for all \(s \in [0, 1]\) * \(H(s, 1) = g(s)\) for all \(s \in [0, 1]\) * \(H(0, t) = H(1, t) = x_0\) for all \(t \in [0, 1]\)
The elements of the fundamental group \(\pi_1(X, x_0)\) are the homotopy classes of loops, denoted \([f]\).
The group operation is concatenation of loops (running one loop after the other at double speed):
\[(f * g)(s) = \begin{cases} f(2s) & 0 \le s \le \frac{1}{2} \\ g(2s - 1) & \frac{1}{2} \le s \le 1 \end{cases}\]






ImportantAlgebraic Invariants



If two spaces \(X\) and \(Y\) are homeomorphic, their fundamental groups are isomorphic: \[\pi_1(X, x_0) \cong \pi_1(Y, y_0)\] For instance, the fundamental group of a circle \(S^1\) is isomorphic to the group of integers \(\mathbb{Z}\), while the fundamental group of a sphere \(S^2\) is trivial (the group containing only the identity).

title: Visualizing Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
text:
In linear algebra, linear transformations act on vectors, stretching, rotating, or shearing them. However, for every linear transformation, there exist special vectors that do not change their direction—only their scale. These are eigenvectors, and their corresponding scaling factors are eigenvalues.

The Mathematical Definition
Let \(A\) be an \(n \times n\) matrix. A non-zero vector \(v\) is an eigenvector of \(A\) if:
\[A v = \lambda v\]
where \(\lambda\) is a scalar known as the eigenvalue corresponding to the eigenvector \(v\).


Geometric Intuition
Imagine a transformation matrix \(A\) applied to a grid of vectors in \(\mathbb{R}^2\):
\[A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\]
If we multiply this matrix by a general vector, the vector changes both length and direction. However, if we choose the vector \(v = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\):
\[A v = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} = 3 \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\]
Here, the direction remains identical along the diagonal, and the vector is scaled by a factor of \(\lambda = 3\).






NoteKey Takeaway



Eigenvectors define the “characteristic directions” along which a linear transformation acts purely by scaling.


In python, we can easily find eigenvalues and eigenvectors using numpy.linalg.eig:


Show code
import numpy as np

A = np.array([[2, 1], [1, 2]])
eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eig(A)

print("Eigenvalues:", eigenvalues)
print("Eigenvectors:\n", eigenvectors)


Eigenvalues: [3. 1.]
Eigenvectors:
 [[ 0.70710678 -0.70710678]
 [ 0.70710678  0.70710678]]

title: Tiago Verissimo
text:
I am a PhD Student in Mathematics at Newcastle University, working in Multivariate Truncated Moment Problems at the moment (no pun intended). I plan to work in different things in the future, but who knows.
Here, you’ll find explorations in pure, applied and computational mathematics. Sometimes I will go into other topics such as computer science, philosophy and mathematical pedagogy.

title: The Beauty of Lebesgue Integration
text:
In classical calculus, the Riemann integral is defined by partitioning the domain (the \(x\)-axis) into small intervals and taking the sum of the areas of the resulting rectangles. While highly intuitive, the Riemann integral fails for highly discontinuous functions, such as the Dirichlet function.
The Lebesgue integral solves this by partitioning the range (the \(y\)-axis) instead of the domain.

Riemann vs. Lebesgue: An Analogy
Suppose you have a pile of coins of different values and want to count them: * Riemann Approach: You pick up the coins one by one in the order they lie on the table, adding each coin’s value to the running total. * Lebesgue Approach: You sort the coins by value (e.g., all \(\$1\) coins together, all \(\$2\) coins together) and then calculate the total sum by multiplying each value by the number of coins of that value.


Mathematical Formulation
Let \((X, \mathcal{M}, \mu)\) be a measure space. For a simple function \(s(x) = \sum_{i=1}^n a_i \chi_{A_i}(x)\) (where \(\chi_{A_i}\) is the indicator function of the measurable set \(A_i\)), the Lebesgue integral is defined as:
\[\int_X s \, d\mu = \sum_{i=1}^n a_i \mu(A_i)\]
For any non-negative measurable function \(f: X \to [0, \infty]\), we define its Lebesgue integral as the supremum of the integrals of simple functions under it:
\[\int_X f \, d\mu = \sup \left\{ \int_X s \, d\mu : 0 \le s \le f, \, s \text{ is simple} \right\}\]






TipLebesgue’s Dominated Convergence Theorem



One of the primary advantages of Lebesgue integration is the ease with which limits and integrals can be interchanged under very mild conditions. If \(f_n \to f\) pointwise, and \(|f_n| \le g\) for a Lebesgue integrable function \(g\), then: \[\lim_{n\to\infty} \int_X f_n \, d\mu = \int_X f \, d\mu\]
